materials.key_property: Intrinsic tensile strength ~130 GPa and Young's modulus ~1 TPa — about 200× stronger than steel by specific strength; carrier mobility >200,000 cm²/V·s; thermal conductivity ~5000 W/m·K.
materials.improvement: Far higher strength-to-weight plus electrical/thermal conductivity than any metal or conventional composite.
materials.uses: Composites, transparent conductive films, sensors, batteries & supercapacitors, RF transistors, membranes.
materials.origin: Isolated by Geim & Novoselov, University of Manchester, 2004 (Nobel Prize 2010).

papers.title: Electric Field Effect in Atomically Thin Carbon Films
The rise of graphene

materials.key_property: Tensile strength up to ~100 GPa, Young's modulus ~1 TPa; metallic or semiconducting depending on chirality.
materials.improvement: ~10–100× stronger than steel at one-sixth the density; conductivity above copper per unit weight.
materials.uses: Conductive films, transistors, structural composites, field emitters, sensors, EMI shielding.
materials.origin: Single-wall tubes reported by Iijima (NEC) & Bethune (IBM), 1993.

papers.title: Single-shell carbon nanotubes of 1-nm diameter

materials.key_property: Tensile strength ~10–60 GPa, modulus ~0.3–1 TPa; telescoping inner walls.
materials.improvement: High stiffness and conductivity at lower cost and higher production volume than SWCNTs.
materials.uses: Conductive plastics, battery-electrode additives, composites, coatings, EMI shielding.
materials.origin: Observed by Sumio Iijima, NEC, 1991.

papers.title: Helical microtubules of graphitic carbon

materials.key_property: Spherical 60-atom carbon cage; strong electron acceptor; superconducting when alkali-doped (K₃C₆₀, Tc ~18 K).
materials.improvement: Molecular electron-acceptor/cage chemistry unavailable in bulk carbon.
materials.uses: Organic photovoltaics, antioxidants/cosmetics, lubricant additives, catalysis, molecular electronics.
materials.origin: Kroto, Curl & Smalley, Rice University, 1985 (Nobel Prize 1996).

papers.title: C60: Buckminsterfullerene
Solid C60: a new form of carbon

materials.formula: C — linear chain, alternating single/triple bonds
materials.key_property: Predicted to be the stiffest/strongest material per unit mass — tensile strength ~200–300 GPa (theory), roughly double graphene; only stable as finite chains, e.g. inside nanotubes.
materials.improvement: Potentially the strongest material per mass known, ~2× graphene's tensile strength.
materials.uses: Potential ultra-strong nanofibres, nanoelectronics, energy storage (largely speculative — unstable in bulk).
materials.origin: Strength predicted at Rice University (Liu et al.), 2013; long chains stabilised in nanotubes, Vienna 2016.

papers.title: Confined linear carbon chains as a route to bulk carbyne

materials.formula: C — 2D sp/sp² network with acetylenic links
materials.key_property: 2D sheet with predicted direction-dependent Dirac cones; stiffness/conductivity near graphene but with an intrinsic, tunable band structure.
materials.improvement: Offers natural band gaps and anisotropic transport that pristine graphene lacks.
materials.uses: Potential semiconductors, anodes, gas-separation membranes.
materials.origin: Predicted by Baughman, Eckhardt & Kertész, 1987; γ-graphyne bulk synthesis 2022 (CU Boulder).

papers.title: Structure-property predictions for new planar forms of carbon

materials.key_property: Uniform sub-nanometre pores, semiconducting, high in-plane mobility and large surface area; first all-carbon material of its kind synthesised at low temperature.
materials.improvement: Tunable band gap and built-in nanopores vs graphene — good for ion transport and catalysis.
materials.uses: Li/Na-battery anodes, electrocatalysis (HER, OER, N₂/CO₂ reduction), gas separation, sensors.
materials.origin: First synthesised by Yuliang Li, Chinese Academy of Sciences (ICCAS), 2010.

papers.title: Architecture of graphdiyne nanoscale films
Graphdiyne: synthesis, properties, and applications

materials.key_property: 1D diamond-like thread; simulated stiffness ~850 GPa and specific tenacity ~4.1×10⁷ N·m/kg, exceeding nanotubes and graphene.
materials.improvement: Combines diamond stiffness with thread flexibility; predicted strength above today's strongest fibres.
materials.uses: Potential ultra-strong lightweight fibres, space-tether-class cables (early stage).
materials.origin: Synthesised by Badding's group, Penn State, Nature Materials 2014.

papers.title: Benzene-derived carbon nanothreads

materials.key_property: Electrically insulating but water-dispersible and chemically functionalisable; weaker than graphene but easily processed into films/membranes.
materials.improvement: Solution-processable and tunable vs pristine graphene; bonds well in composites and membranes.
materials.uses: Water purification/desalination membranes, composite reinforcement, coatings, biomedical carriers, rGO precursor.
materials.origin: Brodie 1859; modern Hummers' method 1958.

papers.title: Preparation of Graphitic Oxide
The chemistry of graphene oxide

materials.formula: C with residual O — partially deoxygenated GO
materials.key_property: Restores much of graphene's conductivity (orders of magnitude above GO) while remaining cheap and scalable.
materials.improvement: A low-cost, scalable route to conductive graphene-like material vs costly pristine graphene.
materials.uses: Supercapacitor/battery electrodes, conductive inks & films, sensors, composites, catalysis supports.
materials.origin: Chemical/thermal reduction of Hummers-type GO; widespread from the late 2000s.

papers.title: Synthesis of graphene-based nanosheets via chemical reduction of exfoliated graphite oxide

materials.key_property: Extreme chemical inertness, gas-impermeable, stable to ~3000 °C in inert atmosphere, low density ~1.4–1.5 g/cm³, biocompatible.
materials.improvement: Glass-like impermeability plus graphite-like conductivity with far better corrosion resistance.
materials.uses: Electrodes, crucibles, high-temperature labware, prosthetic heart valves, semiconductor processing.
materials.origin: Developed by Redfern / Carborundum and others, early 1960s.

papers.title: Fullerene-related structure of commercial glassy carbons

materials.formula: C — graphitic filaments (PAN or pitch precursor)
materials.key_property: Tensile strength ~3.5–7 GPa, modulus ~230–600 GPa, density ~1.75–2.0 g/cm³ — very high specific strength and stiffness.
materials.improvement: Much higher strength- and stiffness-to-weight than steel or aluminium; corrosion-resistant.
materials.uses: Aerospace, automotive, wind-turbine blades, sporting goods, pressure vessels, civil reinforcement.
materials.origin: High-strength PAN fibres developed at RAE Farnborough (UK), 1963.

papers.title: Carbon Fibers: Precursor Systems, Processing, Structure, and Properties

materials.key_property: Reported harder than diamond, ferromagnetic at room temperature, conductive and field-emissive; converts to diamond at ambient pressure.
materials.improvement: Uniquely combines above-diamond hardness with room-temperature ferromagnetism and conductivity.
materials.uses: Ambient-pressure diamond synthesis, hard coatings, field emitters, potential spintronics.
materials.origin: Narayan group, North Carolina State University, 2015.

papers.title: Novel phase of carbon, ferromagnetism, and conversion into diamond

materials.key_property: Metallic (gapless) 2D carbon, unlike semiconducting graphene; predicted modulus ~0.6–0.7 TPa, anisotropic transport.
materials.improvement: Provides intrinsic metallic conduction in an all-carbon sheet, where graphene needs doping/patterning.
materials.uses: Potential conducting wires in carbon-only electronics, anodes, catalysis (proof-of-concept).
materials.origin: Synthesised on a gold surface (Fan, Gottfried et al.), Science 2021.

papers.title: Biphenylene network: A nonbenzenoid carbon allotrope

materials.key_property: Simulations predict up to ~58% harder than cubic diamond; 2025 bulk samples measured Vickers hardness ~114 GPa.
materials.improvement: Potentially harder and stiffer than ordinary diamond along certain directions.
materials.uses: Ultra-hard abrasives, cutting tools, wear coatings (mostly research; natural in impact craters).
materials.origin: First found in the Canyon Diablo meteorite, 1967; pure bulk samples 2025.

papers.title: Hexagonal Diamond — A New Form of Carbon
Lonsdaleite, a Hexagonal Polymorph of Diamond

materials.formula: C — disordered sp²/sp³ (ta-C high-sp³; a-C:H hydrogenated)
materials.key_property: Hardness up to ~40–90 GPa (tetrahedral amorphous carbon), very low friction (~0.05–0.2), inert and smooth.
materials.improvement: Near-diamond hardness and wear/friction as a deposited film at far lower cost/temperature than diamond.
materials.uses: Wear/low-friction coatings on engine parts, tools, razor blades, hard disks, implants, optics.
materials.origin: Diamond-like carbon films first deposited by Aisenberg & Chabot, 1971.

papers.title: Diamond-like amorphous carbon

materials.formula: 3D network of covalently bonded graphene sheets, freeze-dried
materials.key_property: World's lightest solid at synthesis: density ~0.16 mg/cm³ (≈7× lighter than air); ≥99.99% air; absorbs up to ~900× its own weight in oil; elastically recovers after >50% strain.
materials.improvement: Far lighter than silica aerogel and metallic microlattice, yet compressible, conductive and hugely absorbent.
materials.uses: Oil-spill & water/air decontamination, thermal/acoustic insulation, supercapacitor & battery electrodes, pressure sensors, microwave absorption, aerospace lightweighting.
materials.origin: Zhejiang University (Gao Chao group), China, 2013.

papers.title: Multifunctional, Ultra-Flyweight, Synergistically Assembled Carbon Aerogels
Ultralight and Highly Compressible Graphene Aerogels

materials.key_property: Translucent solid; density down to ~1 kg/m³; thermal conductivity ~0.004–0.03 W/m·K — the best solid insulator known; ~1000× less dense than glass.
materials.improvement: Up to ~39× more effective than the best fibreglass; one inch insulates like ~15 panes of glass.
materials.uses: Insulation (buildings, pipelines, spacesuits, Mars rovers), Cherenkov detectors, NASA Stardust dust collector.
materials.origin: First made by Samuel Kistler, 1931; commercialised (Aspen Aerogels, NASA).

papers.title: Coherent Expanded Aerogels and Jellies

materials.key_property: Density ~0.2–1.2 g/cm³, surface area up to ~1200 m²/g, conductive; supercapacitors with ~352 F/g and 100% retention over 65,000 cycles.
materials.improvement: Low weight, high surface area and conductivity outperform many activated-carbon electrodes in cycle stability.
materials.uses: Supercapacitor/battery electrodes, capacitive desalination, catalyst supports, hydrogen storage, EMI shielding.
materials.origin: Developed from organic aerogels by Pekala et al. (LLNL), late 1980s–90s.

papers.title: Organic aerogels from the polycondensation of resorcinol with formaldehyde

materials.formula: Network of hollow porous carbon micro-tubes
materials.key_property: Density as low as ~0.18 mg/cm³; ~99.99% air; compressible up to ~1000× and springs back; withstands ~35× greater force per density than silica aerogel; conductive.
materials.improvement: More robust and resilient than silica aerogels and earlier CNT aerogels at lower density — gains strength under compression.
materials.uses: Battery/supercapacitor electrodes, EMI shielding, lightweight conductive composites, sensors.
materials.origin: TU Hamburg & University of Kiel, Germany, 2012.

papers.title: Aerographite: Ultra Lightweight, Flexible Nanowall, Carbon Microtube Material

materials.formula: Open lattice of hollow Ni-P micro-tubes (~100 nm walls)
materials.key_property: 'World's lightest metal'; density as low as ~0.9 mg/cm³ (~100× lighter than Styrofoam); ~99.99% air; fully recovers after >50% compression.
materials.improvement: Ordered architecture gives far higher energy absorption and recoverable compression than random metal foams.
materials.uses: Aerospace structural cores, impact/energy absorption, acoustic/vibration damping, battery electrodes, insulation.
materials.origin: HRL Laboratories with UC Irvine & Caltech, 2011.

papers.title: Ultralight Metallic Microlattices

materials.key_property: Ultralow density (record ~8.1 kg/m³, porosity ~99.4%); thermal conductivity ~32–38 mW/m·K; biodegradable; >90% recovery after repeated compression.
materials.improvement: Sustainable, biodegradable alternative to petrochemical foams and brittle silica aerogels with competitive insulation.
materials.uses: Sustainable building insulation, oil/water separation, packaging, biomedical scaffolds, filtration.
materials.origin: Multiple groups (KTH, Aalto…), 2000s–2010s.

papers.title: Long and entangled native cellulose I nanofibers allow flexible aerogels

materials.key_property: Up to ~500× stronger than silica aerogel with comparable insulation; flexible/foldable films; withstands ≥~1100 °C without pore collapse.
materials.improvement: Mechanically robust and flexible, unlike brittle, dusty silica aerogels, with high R-values.
materials.uses: Spacecraft & EVA-suit insulation, antennas/substrates, pipeline & building insulation, insulated clothing.
materials.origin: NASA Glenn Research Center, ~2011; licensed commercially.

papers.title: Mechanically Strong, Flexible Polyimide Aerogels Cross-Linked with Aromatic Triamine

materials.key_property: Density ~0.1–1.4 mg/cm³ (can be less dense than air); superelastic (~95% strain); thermal superinsulation ~2.4 mW/m·K in vacuum; stable to ~900 °C in air.
materials.improvement: Electrically insulating yet ultralight and oxidation-resistant where carbon aerogels would burn.
materials.uses: High-temperature super-insulation, IR/thermal stealth, fire-resistant insulation, catalyst supports.
materials.origin: Academic groups, ~2015 onward.

papers.title: Ultralight boron nitride aerogels via template-assisted chemical vapor deposition

materials.key_property: Fracture toughness >200 MPa·m½ at 77 K (up to ~459 at 20 K); tensile strength rises from 763 MPa (RT) to 1280 MPa at 77 K while ductility also rises.
materials.improvement: A single-phase alloy that gets both stronger AND tougher when cooled to cryogenic temperatures, via nanoscale twinning — defeating the usual trade-off.
materials.uses: Cryogenic structures, LNG and space hardware, damage-tolerant load-bearing parts (research).
materials.origin: Introduced by Cantor (UK) and Yeh (Taiwan), 2004; cryo toughness by Ritchie/LBNL, 2014.

papers.title: Microstructural development in equiatomic multicomponent alloys
A fracture-resistant high-entropy alloy for cryogenic applications

materials.key_property: Crack-growth toughness exceeding 400 MPa·m½ at 20 K — among the highest damage tolerance ever measured for any material.
materials.improvement: Out-performs the 5-element Cantor alloy; sustained twinning gives record damage tolerance to liquid-helium temperatures.
materials.uses: Extreme-cryogenic & aerospace structures, fusion/superconducting-magnet hardware (research).
materials.origin: Gludovatz, George & Ritchie, LBNL/ORNL, 2016 (20 K record: Science 2022).

papers.title: Exceptional damage-tolerance of a medium-entropy alloy CrCoNi at cryogenic temperatures

materials.key_property: Retains yield strength ~477 MPa at 1600 °C; room-temperature hardness ~5.25 GPa; single-phase BCC.
materials.improvement: Resists high-temperature softening far better than Ni-based superalloys above ~800 °C.
materials.uses: Next-gen turbine, hypersonic, nuclear and rocket-nozzle high-temperature structures (research).
materials.origin: Senkov et al., US Air Force Research Laboratory, 2010.

papers.title: Refractory high-entropy alloys
Mechanical properties of NbMoTaW and VNbMoTaW refractory high-entropy alloys

materials.key_property: Compressive strength ~772 MPa at 1000 °C; density 7.4 g/cm³; hardness 5.8 GPa; nanoscale basketweave structure mimicking γ/γ′ superalloys.
materials.improvement: One of the first refractory HEAs with high-temperature strength superior to Ni-based superalloys.
materials.uses: High-temperature turbine and aerospace structural alloys (research).
materials.origin: Senkov et al., AFRL, 2016.

papers.title: Microstructure and Properties of Aluminum-Containing Refractory High-Entropy Alloys

materials.key_property: Density only 2.67 g/cm³ with hardness up to 5.9 GPa — strength-to-weight comparable to ceramics.
materials.improvement: Aluminium-like density combined with ceramic-class specific strength, far above conventional light alloys.
materials.uses: Potential aerospace & transport lightweighting (lab stage; contains costly Sc).
materials.origin: Youssef & Koch, North Carolina State University, 2014.

papers.title: A Novel Low-Density, High-Hardness, High-entropy Alloy with Close-packed Single-phase Nanocrystalline Structures

materials.key_property: After cryo-rolling/annealing: yield ~1437 MPa, UTS ~1562 MPa, elongation ~14%; additive-made variants reach UTS ~1620–1737 MPa.
materials.improvement: Self-organised lamellar eutectic gives high strength AND ductility plus the castability single-phase HEAs lack.
materials.uses: Structural castings and additively-manufactured high-performance parts (research).
materials.origin: Lu, Liaw et al. (2014); strength-ductility work 2018–2020.

papers.title: A Promising New Class of High-Temperature Alloys: Eutectic High-Entropy Alloys

materials.key_property: Superhardness: (TiZrNbVHf)N ~63 GPa; (AlTiNbCrZr)N ~46 GPa — far above ~33 GPa for TiAlN.
materials.improvement: Single-phase high-entropy nitride films give superhard, wear- and oxidation-resistant coatings beyond TiAlN.
materials.uses: Cutting-tool, mould and wear-resistant hard coatings (sputter-deposited).
materials.origin: Multiple groups, ~2004 onward.

papers.title: Preparation and characterization of AlCrTaTiZr multi-element nitride coatings

materials.key_property: Yield strength ~1.9 GPa, elastic strain ~2% (~3× crystalline alloys), strength-to-weight >2× Ti/Al/Mg; casts amorphous in cm-thick sections.
materials.improvement: First easily-castable bulk metallic glass — very high strength & resilience plus near-net-shape moulding.
materials.uses: Golf-club faces, watch & phone casings, precision and defence parts (Liquidmetal/Materion).
materials.origin: Peker & Johnson, Caltech, 1993.

papers.title: A highly processable metallic glass: Zr41.2Ti13.8Cu12.5Ni10.0Be22.5

materials.key_property: Combines ~1.5 GPa yield strength with fracture toughness exceeding ~200 MPa·m½ — rivalling the toughest crystalline metals.
materials.improvement: Extensive shear-band plasticity blunts cracks, overcoming the usual brittleness of metallic glasses.
materials.uses: Demonstrator for ultra-damage-tolerant glasses; biocompatible implants (Pd is costly).
materials.origin: Demetriou, Launey, Ritchie & Johnson, Caltech/LBNL, Nature Materials 2011.

papers.title: A damage-tolerant glass

materials.key_property: Hugoniot elastic limit ~8.6–11.8 GPa (a shock metric) and hardness above HV1200 (~2× ultra-high-strength steel); strong glass former.
materials.improvement: Extremely hard, high-elastic-limit iron glass with far better corrosion resistance than crystalline stainless.
materials.uses: Armour, wear/erosion- and corrosion-resistant coatings, nuclear-waste-container coatings (Navy/DOE).
materials.origin: DARPA Structural Amorphous Metals program, ~2000s.

papers.title: Fe-based bulk metallic glasses with diameter thickness larger than one centimeter

materials.key_property: Saturation induction 1.56 T, very high permeability and very low core loss; ribbon ~23 µm thick.
materials.improvement: No grain boundaries means dramatically lower transformer core loss than grain-oriented silicon steel.
materials.uses: Energy-efficient distribution-transformer cores, inductors, sensors, actuators.
materials.origin: Allied Signal/Metglas (melt-spinning by Pond & Duwez, Caltech, 1960).

materials.key_property: Equiatomic high-entropy glass castable amorphous to >15 mm diameter — among the best glass-forming of any HE-BMG.
materials.improvement: Merges the high-mixing-entropy concept with the amorphous structure, broadening glass-forming compositions.
materials.uses: Bulk amorphous structural & precision components (research).
materials.origin: Reported ~2014.

papers.title: High mixing entropy bulk metallic glasses

materials.key_property: Ultimate tensile strength ~2070 MPa (300 ksi) via age-hardened nanoscale intermetallic precipitates.
materials.improvement: High strength with good fracture toughness; near-zero carbon avoids brittleness of quench-hardened steels.
materials.uses: Rocket-motor casings, aerospace structural parts, high-performance gears & shafts, tooling.
materials.origin: International Nickel Company (INCO), 1960s.

papers.title: Modeling of precipitation hardening during the aging and overaging of 18Ni-Co-Mo-Ti maraging 300 steel

materials.key_property: Yield strength ~2400 MPa, UTS exceeding ~2500 MPa after heat treatment — among the strongest commercial maraging grades.
materials.improvement: Higher Co and Ti drive extra precipitation strengthening over grade 300.
materials.uses: High-load aerospace & defence components, ultra-high-strength tooling.
materials.origin: INCO maraging family, 1960s–70s.

papers.title: Precipitation in 18 wt% Ni maraging steel of grade 350

materials.key_property: Tensile strength typically >1000 MPa with elongation >50%; deformation twins act as moving grain boundaries.
materials.improvement: Exceptional strength-ductility combination far beyond conventional automotive steels; high energy absorption.
materials.uses: Automotive crash-absorbing structures, body-in-white, lightweighting.
materials.origin: High-Mn austenitic concept advanced 1990s–2000s (Grässel/Frommeyer).

papers.title: High strength Fe-Mn-(Al, Si) TRIP/TWIP steels development
High manganese austenitic twinning induced plasticity steels: A review
papers.venue: Curr. Opin. Solid State Mater. Sci.

materials.formula: High-C, high-Si Fe-C-Si-Mn-Cr; <100 nm bainitic ferrite plates
materials.key_property: Tensile strength up to ~2.4 GPa with ~15% ductility; bainitic-ferrite plates under 100 nm thick formed by low-temperature isothermal transformation — no rapid quenching.
materials.improvement: Bulk nanoscale microstructure at ambient pressure; very high strength plus toughness from retained austenite.
materials.uses: Armour plate, bearings, wear-resistant high-strength parts.
materials.origin: Bhadeshia & Caballero, University of Cambridge, ~2000–2004.

papers.title: Very strong bainite
papers.venue: Curr. Opin. Solid State Mater. Sci.

papers.title: Nanostructured bainite

materials.key_property: Strength up to ~2.5–2.6 GPa — roughly ten times stronger than conventional coarse-grained stainless — via nanotwins, C segregation and nanograins.
materials.improvement: Among the highest strengths reported for any steel, combining several nanoscale strengthening mechanisms.
materials.uses: Research benchmark for ultra-high-strength structural and protective materials.
materials.origin: Demonstrated via high-pressure torsion, Nature Communications 2018.

papers.title: Strengthening materials by engineering coherent internal boundaries at the nanoscale

materials.formula: Medium-Mn steel, warm+cold rolled and annealed
materials.key_property: Yield strength ~2.2 GPa with ~16% uniform elongation; high dislocation density raises both strength and ductility.
materials.improvement: Best yield-strength/uniform-elongation combination among high-strength metals at ~1/5 the cost of incumbent aerospace steel.
materials.uses: Potential aerospace & defence structures, lightweight high-strength components.
materials.origin: HKU-led team (M.X. Huang et al.), Science 2017.

papers.title: High dislocation density-induced large ductility in deformed and partitioned steels

materials.key_property: Minimum yield strength 1300 MPa (188 ksi) — among the strongest off-the-shelf structural plate.
materials.improvement: Far higher yield than ordinary structural steel, enabling major weight savings in load-bearing structures.
materials.uses: Crane booms, lifting & handling equipment, trailers, heavy machine frames.
materials.origin: SSAB (brand 2015; lineage from Weldox/Domex).

materials.key_property: Work-hardens dramatically under impact (surface to ~550 BHN) while the core stays tough — extreme abrasion/impact wear resistance.
materials.improvement: Self-hardening surface under service loads; modern high-Mn grades add cryogenic toughness.
materials.uses: Rock-crusher jaws, rail crossings/frogs, excavator teeth, LNG/cryogenic structures.
materials.origin: Invented by Sir Robert Hadfield, 1882.

papers.title: Hadfield's Manganese Steel
Hadfield manganese austenitic steel: a review of manufacturing processes and properties

materials.key_property: Creep & oxidation resistance to ~1150 °C; rhenium gives ~28 °C capability gain over 1st-generation alloys; ~70% γ′ volume fraction.
materials.improvement: Single-crystal casting eliminates grain boundaries, hugely improving creep life vs polycrystalline superalloys.
materials.uses: Gas-turbine and jet-engine turbine blades and vanes.
materials.origin: Cannon-Muskegon Corporation, 2nd-generation single-crystal alloy (1980s).

papers.title: Development of two rhenium-containing superalloys for single-crystal blade and directionally solidified vane applications

materials.formula: Ni-base single crystal, ~6% Re, high refractory, low Cr
materials.key_property: Highest creep-rupture resistance of nickel single crystals; ~30 °C stronger than 2nd-gen; density up to ~9.1 g/cm³.
materials.improvement: Doubled rhenium pushes temperature capability higher (trade-off: TCP-phase risk, higher density).
materials.uses: Most demanding hot-section turbine blades in advanced aero engines.
materials.origin: CMSX-10 (Cannon-Muskegon) & René N6 (GE), mid-1990s.

papers.title: René N6: Third Generation Single Crystal Superalloy

materials.formula: Fe-Cr-Al + nanoscale Y₂O₃ dispersoids (e.g. MA956, PM2000)
materials.key_property: Retains strength and oxidation resistance to ~1200 °C; nanoscale yttria dispersoids pin dislocations and grain boundaries.
materials.improvement: High-temperature creep strength and radiation-damage resistance beyond conventional superalloys/steels.
materials.uses: Nuclear (fission/fusion) structural components, high-temperature heat exchangers and combustors.
materials.origin: Mechanically-alloyed ODS family (INCO MA956, Plansee PM2000), 1970s–90s.

papers.title: Dispersion strengthened superalloys by mechanical alloying

materials.key_property: Metallic conductivity up to ~15,000 S/cm; volumetric capacitance ~900 F/cm³ (hydrogel electrodes up to 1500 F/cm³).
materials.improvement: Metal-like conductivity with a hydrophilic, tunable surface and far higher volumetric capacitance than activated carbon.
materials.uses: Supercapacitors, Li/Na-ion electrodes, EMI shielding, water desalination, gas/biosensors, transparent conductors.
materials.origin: Drexel University (Gogotsi & Barsoum; Naguib), 2011.

papers.title: Two-Dimensional Nanocrystals Produced by Exfoliation of Ti3AlC2
2D metal carbides and nitrides (MXenes) for energy storage

materials.key_property: ~3× stiffer than titanium yet machinable with a hacksaw; recovers fully from ~1 GPa compression at room temperature.
materials.improvement: Unlike brittle ceramics it is machinable, thermal-shock- and damage-tolerant while keeping ceramic stiffness.
materials.uses: High-temperature structural parts, heating elements, electrical contacts, MXene precursor.
materials.origin: Drexel University, Barsoum & El-Raghy, first dense synthesis 1996.

papers.title: Synthesis and Characterization of a Remarkable Ceramic: Ti3SiC2
The MN+1AXN phases: A new class of solids

materials.key_property: Machinable, creep- and fatigue-resistant; forms a dense, self-healing alumina scale giving excellent oxidation resistance above ~1000 °C.
materials.improvement: Self-healing alumina scale gives oxidation resistance approaching alumina formers, while staying conductive and machinable.
materials.uses: High-temperature oxidation-resistant coatings, heating elements, burner nozzles, MXene precursor.
materials.origin: MAX-phase class (Barsoum et al.), studied from the late 1990s.

papers.title: Processing and characterization of Ti2AlC, Ti2AlN, and Ti2AlC0.5N0.5

materials.key_property: Metallic 2D sheet, more flexible than graphene (lowest reported bending stiffness); predicted specific strength above graphene; theoretical H₂ storage >15 wt%.
materials.improvement: Lightest experimentally realised 2D metal — flexible, metallic, with high theoretical hydrogen storage.
materials.uses: Flexible electronics/interconnects, battery anodes, hydrogen storage, composite reinforcement.
materials.origin: First confirmed 2015 on Ag(111) (Argonne/Northwestern and a separate team).

papers.title: Synthesis of borophenes: Anisotropic, two-dimensional boron polymorphs

materials.key_property: Wide-bandgap insulator ~5.9–6.4 eV; atomically-thin h-BN reaches ~751 W/m·K thermal conductivity; ~2% lattice mismatch with graphene.
materials.improvement: An atomically-flat, defect-free dielectric that dramatically boosts 2D-device mobility vs SiO₂; excellent thermal/chemical stability.
materials.uses: Dielectric substrate/encapsulation for 2D devices, deep-UV emitters, high-temperature lubricant, ceramics.
materials.origin: Bulk h-BN long known; atomically-thin form studied from the late 2000s.

papers.title: Boron nitride substrates for high-quality graphene electronics

materials.key_property: Buckled honeycomb silicon; a room-temperature field-effect transistor was demonstrated in 2015; tunable bandgap ~0–0.1 eV.
materials.improvement: Silicon-compatible 2D analogue of graphene with a tunable bandgap — potentially integrates with existing CMOS.
materials.uses: Field-effect & tunnel transistors, low-power electronics, hydrogen storage, supercapacitors.
materials.origin: First observed 2010 on silver; FET demonstrated 2015.

papers.title: Silicene: Compelling Experimental Evidence for Graphenelike Two-Dimensional Silicon

materials.key_property: Direct, thickness-tunable bandgap ~0.3 eV (bulk) to ~1.88 eV (monolayer); high carrier mobility; strongly anisotropic puckered structure.
materials.improvement: Fills the gap between zero-gap graphene and large-gap TMDs, with high mobility and useful in-plane anisotropy.
materials.uses: Transistors, photodetectors/optoelectronics, IR devices, battery anodes (limited by air degradation).
materials.origin: Bulk black phosphorus 1914; monolayer isolated 2014.

papers.title: Black phosphorus field-effect transistors
Phosphorene: An Unexplored 2D Semiconductor with a High Hole Mobility

materials.key_property: Direct ~1.8 eV bandgap in the monolayer; first single-layer transistor (2011) had on/off ratio ~10⁸; a 2D MoS₂ microprocessor was built in 2017.
materials.improvement: Provides a real semiconducting bandgap and high on/off switching that gapless graphene lacks.
materials.uses: Transistors & microprocessors, photodetectors, hydrogen-evolution catalysis, dry lubricant, flexible electronics.
materials.origin: Natural molybdenite; first monolayer FET 2011 (EPFL, Kis group).

papers.title: Single-layer MoS2 transistors

materials.formula: C — bilayer graphene → diamond-like film under impact
materials.key_property: Two graphene layers that transiently become as stiff as bulk diamond under room-temperature impact, then revert.
materials.improvement: Switches from foil-light flexible film to harder-than-diamond on impact — far lighter than rigid diamond coatings.
materials.uses: Ultralight wear-resistant coatings, flexible bulletproof films, pressure-switchable electronics.
materials.origin: CUNY Advanced Science Research Center (Riedo group), Nature Nanotechnology 2017–2018.

papers.title: Ultrahard carbon film from epitaxial two-layer graphene

materials.key_property: Density ~4.0 g/cm³ (about half that of nickel superalloys) while keeping strength and oxidation resistance up to ~1000 °C.
materials.improvement: Near half the weight of nickel superalloys at comparable high-temperature performance (drawback: low room-temperature ductility).
materials.uses: Low-pressure jet-engine turbine blades (GE GEnx on Boeing 787/747-8), automotive turbocharger wheels.
materials.origin: First large-scale jet-engine use in GE GEnx, in service 2011.

papers.title: Intermetallic alloys based on gamma titanium aluminide

materials.key_property: Anomalous yield strength that rises with temperature up to ~600–700 °C; melting point ~1385 °C; good oxidation resistance.
materials.improvement: Strengthens superalloys (as the γ′ precipitate) far better at high temperature than disordered alloys.
materials.uses: γ′ strengthening phase in nickel superalloys; furnace fixtures, dies, high-temperature parts (B-doped for ductility).
materials.origin: Ductilised polycrystalline Ni₃Al developed at Oak Ridge (C.T. Liu, 1980s).

papers.title: Ductility improvement of Ni3Al by boron addition
Effect of boron on grain-boundaries in Ni3Al

materials.formula: WC-Co — carbide grains in a cobalt binder
materials.key_property: Vickers hardness ~1200–2200 HV with modulus ~450–650 GPa, tuned by cobalt content and WC grain size.
materials.improvement: Combines extreme carbide hardness/wear resistance with cobalt-binder toughness — vastly outlasting tool steels.
materials.uses: Cutting inserts, milling/turning tools, mining & drill bits, dies, wear parts.
materials.origin: Cemented carbide commercialised in the 1920s–30s (Krupp/WIDIA); nanograined grades from the 1990s.

papers.title: Cemented carbide microstructures: a review
papers.venue: Int. J. Refract. Met. Hard Mater.

materials.formula: Al matrix with sealed gas-filled pores (~0.1–0.6 g/cm³)
materials.key_property: Density 5–25% of solid aluminium with high specific stiffness and excellent crash energy absorption at near-constant plateau stress.
materials.improvement: Better stiffness-to-weight and impact absorption than solid sheet; non-combustible vs polymer foam.
materials.uses: Automotive crash absorbers, lightweight sandwich panels, sound/vibration damping, biomedical scaffolds.
materials.origin: Commercialised 1990s–2000s (Alulight/Cymat/Alporas).

papers.title: Manufacture, characterisation and application of cellular metals and metal foams

materials.key_property: Up to ~80–97% porosity with continuous open pores — very high surface area and permeability.
materials.improvement: Combines structural support with through-flow heat exchange and filtration impossible in solid metal.
materials.uses: Heat exchangers/sinks, catalyst supports, filters, electrodes, flame arrestors.
materials.origin: Developed mid-20th century onward (e.g. Duocel by ERG Aerospace).

papers.title: Manufacture, characterisation and application of cellular metals and metal foams

materials.key_property: Closed-porosity composite with tailorable low density (~0.4–0.7 g/cm³) plus high compressive and hydrostatic crush strength.
materials.improvement: Better compressive and pressure resistance per weight than open foams; buoyant at depth.
materials.uses: Deep-sea buoyancy modules and ROV floats, aerospace cores, marine & defence structures.
materials.origin: Developed in the 1960s for deep-sea buoyancy.

papers.title: Applications of Polymer Matrix Syntactic Foams

materials.formula: Polycrystalline metal with grain size <100 nm
materials.key_property: Hardness and yield strength several times the coarse-grained counterpart via Hall-Petch refinement (e.g. nanocrystalline Ni up to ~6× harder).
materials.improvement: Much higher strength, wear and corrosion resistance than conventional microcrystalline metals.
materials.uses: Wear-resistant coatings, sporting goods, MEMS, structural plating (e.g. Integran Nanovate).
materials.origin: Field pioneered in the 1980s (Gleiter); commercialised by Integran.

papers.title: Mechanical properties of nanocrystalline materials

materials.formula: Pure Cu with high-density coherent nanotwins (~15 nm spacing)
materials.key_property: Tensile strength up to ~10× coarse-grained copper while keeping near-pure-copper electrical conductivity.
materials.improvement: Breaks the usual strength-vs-conductivity trade-off — twin boundaries block dislocations but barely scatter electrons.
materials.uses: High-performance interconnects, electrodes, electrical contacts, MEMS.
materials.origin: Lu et al., Institute of Metal Research (Shenyang), Science 2004 & 2009.

papers.title: Ultrahigh Strength and High Electrical Conductivity in Copper

materials.key_property: Low Young's modulus ~40 GPa with strength >1100 MPa and ~2.5% elastic strain; deforms with little or no work hardening.
materials.improvement: Far lower modulus and higher elastic deformability than conventional Ti alloys — trace oxygen enables near dislocation-free deformation.
materials.uses: Biomedical implants (bone-matching stiffness), eyeglass frames, springs, precision instruments.
materials.origin: Toyota Central R&D Labs, 2003 (Saito et al., Science).

papers.title: Multifunctional Alloys Obtained via a Dislocation-Free Plastic Deformation Mechanism

materials.key_property: Density as low as ~1.35 g/cm³ — the lightest structural metallic alloys (lighter than Mg ~1.74 and Al ~2.7).
materials.improvement: Lower density and improved formability vs conventional Mg/Al alloys; high specific stiffness.
materials.uses: Aerospace & satellite structures, electronics housings, armour, portable-device frames.
materials.origin: Developed mid-20th century; LA141 in US aerospace since the 1960s.

papers.title: Recent progress in magnesium-lithium alloys

materials.key_property: Liquid at room temperature (eutectic melts ~11 °C; branded Galinstan ~−19 °C); boils >1300 °C; non-toxic, mercury-free; conductive.
materials.improvement: A mercury-free, reconfigurable, self-healing liquid-metal conductor with high thermal conductivity.
materials.uses: Stretchable/reconfigurable electronics, soft robotics, thermometers, CPU thermal interface, antennas.
materials.origin: Galinstan trademarked by Geratherm Medical (1990s).

papers.title: Thermophysical Properties of the Liquid Ga-In-Sn Eutectic Alloy

materials.key_property: Raises shape-memory transformation temperatures well above conventional NiTi (~100 °C) toward ~500 °C, with work output above 500 MPa.
materials.improvement: Extends usable shape-memory/superelastic temperature range far beyond binary NiTi for hot environments.
materials.uses: Aerospace actuators, turbomachinery damping, high-temperature & automotive actuators.
materials.origin: NASA Glenn Research Center and academic studies, 2010s.

papers.title: NiTiHf-based shape memory alloys

materials.key_property: Record surface area for an alkaline-earth carbonate (~800 m²/g); adsorbs more water at low humidity than zeolites and regenerates at low energy.
materials.improvement: Long thought impossible to make anhydrous; out-performs zeolites for low-humidity moisture control.
materials.uses: Desiccant/moisture control for electronics & pharma, drug delivery, spill cleanup.
materials.origin: Uppsala University, Sweden, 2013 (Forsgren, Strømme et al.).

papers.title: A Template-Free, Ultra-Adsorbing, High Surface Area Carbonate Nanostructure

materials.formula: Carbon fibres in epoxy/thermoplastic; CNT/graphene hybrids
materials.key_property: Very high specific strength and stiffness — ~5× stronger than steel at roughly one-fifth the density in the fibre direction.
materials.improvement: Large weight savings over aluminium/steel; nanofiller hybrids add interlaminar toughness and conductivity.
materials.uses: Aircraft (Boeing 787 ~50% composite), spacecraft, automotive, wind-turbine blades, sporting goods.
materials.origin: CFRP since the 1960s; nano-hybrid and thin-ply advances 2000s–2010s.

papers.title: Review on carbon fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) and their mechanical performance

materials.key_property: 3D-printed structures that change shape or function over time in response to heat/stress via a programmed shape-memory transformation.
materials.improvement: Enables complex geometries and spatially graded transformation impossible with conventional SMA machining.
materials.uses: Self-deploying aerospace structures, soft robotics, biomedical stents, adaptive actuators.
materials.origin: Concept coined by Skylar Tibbits (MIT) ~2013; metal SMA additive work at NASA and universities since.

papers.title: Active materials by four-dimension printing
Multi-shape active composites by 3D printing of digital shape memory polymers

materials.key_property: Claimed to resist a blowtorch / simulated nuclear flash to thousands of degrees with negligible damage via an intumescent char layer — never peer-reviewed or independently reproduced.
materials.improvement: Claimed dramatically better thermal/ablation resistance than known barriers, but UNVERIFIED.
materials.uses: Claimed thermal/fire protection, re-entry shielding; no verified commercial product.
materials.origin: Maurice Ward (UK), publicised ~1990 on BBC; formula lost on his death (2011). Status unverified.

materials.formula: Hydrogen in a conductive metallic phase under extreme pressure (~495 GPa claimed)
materials.key_property: Predicted to be a metal and possible room-temperature superconductor; a 2017 claim reported a reflective phase at ~495 GPa, widely disputed and the sample lost.
materials.improvement: Theoretical ultra-high-energy-density propellant and potential room-temperature superconductor.
materials.uses: Theoretical: rocket propellant, superconductors, fundamental high-pressure physics.
materials.origin: Predicted by Wigner & Huntington 1935; 2017 Harvard claim (Dias & Silvera), disputed.

papers.title: Observation of the Wigner-Huntington transition to metallic hydrogen
